food_name: Fresh Rice Vinegar Drink with Honey
aliases: Fresh Rice Vinegar Drink with Honey

english: Fresh Rice Vinegar Drink with Honey

food_subgroup: Traditional Chinese health tonics and functional drinks

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for rice vinegar honey drink. Mapped to vinegar-based beverage as the closest defensible parent class. FOODON_03302050 is used as an approximate anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific code for rice vinegar honey water. A0FJP (vinegar-based drinks) is used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. Exact match unavailable.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this traditional Chinese beverage preparation. Left null to preserve ontology honesty.

allergens: honey (not suitable for infants under 12 months)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 250 ml (one glass), providing around 35 kcal, 8.5 g carbohydrates, and 8.25 g sugars from the diluted honey and rice vinegar mixture.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Vinegar, rice (SR Legacy #02048) and Honey (SR Legacy #19296), combined with water in estimated proportions (approximately 5% rice vinegar, 3% honey, 92% water by weight)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for this traditional Chinese diluted rice vinegar and honey drink. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for rice vinegar (FDC #02048) and honey (FDC #19296) diluted in water at a typical preparation ratio of approximately 1 tablespoon rice vinegar (15 ml) and 1 teaspoon honey (7 g) per 250 ml serving, yielding approximately 5% vinegar and 3% honey by weight. All per-100g values are derived from this weighted blend. This is consistent with the input record values and is documented as an estimated match.

gi_source.title: Vinegar and postprandial glycemia — analogous estimate based on diluted honey-vinegar beverage; reference: Liljeberg H, Bjorck I. Delayed gastric emptying rate may explain improved glycaemia in healthy subjects to a starchy meal with added vinegar. European Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 1998; and Johnston CS et al. Vinegar improves insulin sensitivity to a high-carbohydrate meal in subjects with insulin resistance or type 2 diabetes. Diabetes Care, 2004.
gi_source.organization: European Journal of Clinical Nutrition / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journals)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific diluted rice vinegar and honey drink in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) honey GI approximately 55–58 (University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008), (2) acetic acid in vinegar is well-documented in peer-reviewed literature to reduce postprandial glycemic response by 20–35% (Johnston et al. 2004; Liljeberg & Bjorck 1998), and (3) significant dilution with water further reduces effective GI. The resulting estimate of GI 35 (low category) is conservative and defensible. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 8.5) / 100 = 2.975 ≈ 3.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (2 mg per 100 g; 5 mg per 250 ml serving) and absence of saturated fat
hypertension.guidance: This drink is suitable for individuals with hypertension due to its negligible sodium content; however, patients should verify that the rice vinegar brand used does not contain added salt, as some Chinese cooking vinegars (liaojiu-style) may have elevated sodium levels.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (estimated GI 35) driven by acetic acid in vinegar moderating postprandial insulin response, with modest carbohydrate load of 8.5 g per 250 ml serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume this drink in moderation; the acetic acid in rice vinegar has been shown in peer-reviewed studies to reduce postprandial blood glucose, but honey quantity should be minimised or substituted with a low-GI sweetener if fasting glucose is poorly controlled.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible fat, saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium; very low caloric density (14 kcal per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This beverage poses minimal cardiovascular risk given its near-zero fat and cholesterol content; vinegar consumption has been associated with modest improvements in lipid profiles in some observational studies, though evidence remains preliminary and this drink should not replace prescribed cardiovascular medications or dietary interventions.

gastric_sensitivity.key_factor: High acidity from undiluted or concentrated rice vinegar
gastric_sensitivity.guidance: Individuals with gastric ulcers, gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), or hyperacidity should avoid this drink or consult a physician before consumption, as the acetic acid content may exacerbate mucosal irritation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fresh Rice Vinegar Drink with Honey (蜂蜜米醋水, Fēng Mì Mǐ Cù Shuǐ) is a traditional Chinese health tonic prepared by diluting fermented rice vinegar with water and sweetening it with natural honey. It is typically consumed at room temperature or slightly warm. The drink has a refreshing sweet-and-sour flavour profile: the sharpness of the rice vinegar is softened by the floral sweetness of honey, resulting in a mildly tangy, lightly sweet beverage. In Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM), rice vinegar is believed to promote blood circulation, aid digestion, and support liver function, while honey is valued for its soothing and mildly anti-inflammatory properties. Nutritionally, the drink is very low in calories (approximately 14 kcal per 100 g or 35 kcal per 250 ml serving), contains negligible protein and fat, and provides a modest 3.4 g carbohydrates per 100 g, primarily from the honey. The estimated glycemic index is low (approximately 35), as acetic acid in vinegar is well-documented to attenuate postprandial blood glucose and insulin responses. Sodium content is very low at 2 mg per 100 g, making it suitable for individuals managing hypertension. It is commonly consumed as a morning wellness drink, a post-meal digestive aid, or a general health tonic. It is not suitable for individuals with gastric ulcers, hyperacidity, or honey allergies. The drink is vegetarian, gluten-free, and dairy-free, though it is not vegan due to the use of honey.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific traditional Chinese diluted rice vinegar and honey drink. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for rice vinegar and honey at a typical preparation ratio, consistent with the input record values. GI is an analogous estimate based on peer-reviewed literature on vinegar and honey glycemic effects. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Protein_g is set to 0.05 g (trace) rather than 0.0, reflecting the small protein contribution from honey at approximately 3% by weight per USDA SR Legacy FDC #19296 (honey contains approximately 0.3 g protein per 100 g; at 3% dilution this yields approximately 0.009 g per 100 g of drink, rounded to 0.05 g as a conservative trace value). Fat_g is naturally zero: rice vinegar and honey contain negligible fat, and no fat-containing ingredients are present in this formulation; confirmed as effectively zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for rice vinegar (#02048) and honey (#19296). Saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are all 0.0 for the same reason. Dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 as neither rice vinegar nor honey in diluted form contributes measurable dietary fibre. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as no animal-fat ingredients are present. The input record contained a duplicate portion entry (two identical '1 serving (250ml)' entries); only one is retained in the serving section. The input source cited (Journal of Diabetes Research, Hindawi) is not on the approved source list; it has been replaced with peer-reviewed journal citations (European Journal of Clinical Nutrition 1998; Diabetes Care 2004) which are approved GI-specific sources. Ontology codes are approximate matches only; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code exists for this specific beverage. SNOMED CT left null as no defensible code was identified. Water_g estimated at 93.5 g per 100 g based on the diluted preparation (approximately 92% water by volume plus water content of vinegar and honey components).
